_doc: Golden set for the resolver agent. Each case has an optional `mode` field: 'deterministic' (run only with --llm none), 'llm' (run only with --llm gemini), or 'both' / absent (run in both modes). The runner picks which set to load via the --llm flag — see tests/eval/resolver/run_eval.py. Catalog fixture: tests/eval/resolver/fixtures/catalog.sql layered on top of init_complete_database.sql. Assertion shapes: resolved_sku (exact), resolved_sku_in (any-of, for cases where multiple outcomes are acceptable), has_resolution_error (true).

cases._note: Direct SKU lookup, no fuzzy logic involved. Runs in both modes — exact SKU should never trigger LLM.
User says 'pulsera negra' — must map to BC-BRACELET-BLACK. In deterministic mode the negra→black translation table handles it; in LLM mode the disambiguator picks BLACK from the multi-candidate set.
user_input contains the variant 'dorada' → detect_variant_hints triggers → must resolve to GOLD bracelet.
ADD_STOCK with two items; both must resolve, in the order the router emitted them.

Bare 'pulsera' yields zero candidates from fuzzy_match_with_scores (the word is in the skip list of meaningful_words), so resolve_product_reference fires the standard error. Holds in both modes — branch A0 (no candidates) bypasses the LLM disambiguator entirely.
User says 'arcoiris' — must match BC-BRACELET-RAINBOW via specific descriptor.

User says 'pulsera celeste' — no celeste product. The deterministic path emits resolution_error. Excluded from --llm gemini because Gemini equates celeste≈turquesa and resolves to TURQUOISE (real prod behavior, not a bug — but the case asserts the deterministic behavior, so we run it deterministic-only). The LLM-mode equivalent is llm_disambig_pulsera_xs_picks_some_bracelet, which tests the prompt's bias for non-rejection.
Branch B (single low-confidence). 'collar artesanal clasico' yields one candidate (BC-NECKLACE-CLASSIC, score 0.67). LLM verifies the match.
Branch B (single low-confidence). 'aros silver' yields one candidate (BC-EARRINGS-SILVER, score 0.50) — silver is not in the translation table but the LLM should map it to plateados.

Branch B (single low-confidence). 'collar de plata' yields BC-NECKLACE-CLASSIC (0.50) only because 'collar' overlaps with the necklace's name. The current llm_disambiguate_product prompt does not allow rejection (it always picks one of the candidates), so the LLM falls back to the only candidate. This case asserts that behavior — if the prompt is later updated to permit rejection, this case should flip to has_resolution_error. Tracking issue: see PR description.

Branch B (single low-confidence). 'collar largo' yields BC-NECKLACE-CLASSIC (0.50). Same fall-back behavior as collar_de_plata — LLM cannot reject and picks the only available candidate. See PR description.
Branch C (multi-candidate). 10 candidates with overlap on 'pulsera'; turquesa is the discriminator. LLM must pick TURQUOISE.
Branch C (multi-candidate). LLM must pick PURPLE.
Branch C (multi-candidate). LLM must pick WHITE.

Branch C (multi-candidate). 'pulsera xs' yields all 10 bracelets on 'pulsera' overlap — XS is not a real size variant. The current prompt forces the LLM to pick one. This case asserts it picks *some* bracelet (any of the 10), not a non-bracelet and not error. If the prompt is updated to permit rejection, this case should flip to has_resolution_error. See PR description.
Branch C (multi-candidate). 'pulsera de color' is underspecified. LLM picks some bracelet — any is reasonable.
Branch C (multi-candidate). 'pulsera dorada arcoiris' contains two real but mutually exclusive attributes. LLM should pick one of the two (GOLD or RAINBOW) rather than a third unrelated bracelet.
